Provide merchants and planners with a single, unified planning experience that brings together outputs from the Forecast service and Buy Quantification / Flow services, allowing users to review, understand, and commit plans without navigating multiple disconnected steps.  Merchants and planners lack a single view of the full unit plan. Decisions made in one step are difficult to reconcile in another. Iteration is slow when forecasts or assumptions change. Translating plans into supplier intent and purchase orders is cumbersome. High cognitive load increases execution risk. As a result, planners spend more time navigating tools than making informed decisions.

Enterprise: Merch Tools, Assortment and Space: Redesign long-lead merchandising
Forecast as a Service with Channel and Placement Split

Enable AEX to consume forecasts in a form that supports executable planning today while aligning with Assortment Planning's service based forecasting architecture.  The Forecast service provides demand-channel forecasts, but AEX users still plan, buy, and execute at the fulfillment node level. Without fulfillment-channel visibility in AEX: Buy quantities will be understated. Space planning will be incorrect. Execution teams will compensate manually. Trust in AEX outputs will erode. Demand-only planning represents a future operating model, but the organization is not yet equipped—technically or behaviorally—to adopt it fully.

Buy Quantification and Flow as a Service

Enable AEX to determine buy quantity and how inventory flows through a rule based, service driven system, so planners focus on validating quantities rather than manually constructing flow strategies. The aim is to...  Buy Quantification, flow planning, and size disaggregation occur in multiple steps/screens; planners see different unit counts and must reconcile manually. Planners iterate across flow scenarios to make totals fit receipts, which can cause under/over-buys. Size logic living outside BQ causes rework and confusion when flows are applied.

Enterprise: Merch Tools, Assortment and Space: Redesign long-lead merchandising
AEX Fashion:  Use Assortment Planning's  Plan Creation & Plan List

When the AEX Fashion Line Planning experience was originally built, historical LY and LLY selling data was intended to help merchants make informed Pre-Season assortment decisions by reviewing prior performance, determining how many styles and colors to carry under each fineline, adding those to their current Line Plan in the tool, and then editing TY plan metrics (Pre-Season) or adding incremental styles and colors as needed.  In practice, Fashion merchants rely on enterprise selling history tools such as MerchOne and Scintilla**, outside of AEX Fashion,** to inform high-level assortment breadth, such as how many finelines, styles, and colors they can carry in a season. Those high-level decisions are then shared with Suppliers, Product Development, and Sourcing Partners, and detailed assortment inputs for the season are later provided back to merchants through spreadsheets from Suppliers (primarily National Brands) and internal Product Development and Sourcing partners (primarily Private Label).  Merchants use historical LY and LLY data and/or the “Add Existing” fineline functionality, along with the ability to add styles and colors in AEX Fashion Line Planning, to set up the required fineline, style, and color structure that aligns to these partner inputs and to complete the attributes needed for AEX downstream modules and execution integration, not as the primary source of Pre-Season assortment details.  As a result, Line Planning today requires significant manual consolidation and re-entry of partner inputs, increasing effort and risk of errors, and limiting the ability of AEX Fashion to serve as an integrated starting point for Pre-Season assortment planning.

Enterprise: Merch Tools, Assortment and Space: Redesign long-lead merchandising

Walmart's early-stage product development and evaluation processes are fragmented across multiple systems: - PD develops proprietary/private brand concepts in OneSource, where merchants cannot see early work. - Designers and PD teams develop concepts in Centric, also without merchant visibility. - Merchants operate in AEX or Assortment Planning (AP), lacking visibility into upstream PD/Design intent. - Suppliers submit concepts via Supplier One or email, creating parallel "shadow repositories." Each tool maintains its own version of "potential items," blocking collaboration, duplicating effort, and preventing downstream services from operating on a single, consistent dataset. This delays merchant decisions, creates redundant concept creation, and prevents SKU-level forecasting until very late in the process.

Walmart requires a shared event calendar and lightweight project management system that synchronizes workflows, timelines, and milestones across MerchOne (merchants), OneSource (sourcing/technical), and SupplierOne (suppliers) in order to ensure all users—internal and external—operate on the same sequence of events and critical business milestones tied to PLM, sourcing, and product readiness.

Provide merchants and planners with a single, unified planning experience that brings together outputs from the Forecast service and Buy Quantification / Flow services, allowing users to review, understand, and commit plans without navigating multiple disconnected steps.  Merchants and planners lack a single view of the full unit plan. Decisions made in one step are difficult to reconcile in another. Iteration is slow when forecasts or assumptions change. Translating plans into supplier intent and purchase orders is cumbersome. High cognitive load increases execution risk. As a result, planners spend more time navigating tools than making informed decisions.

Enable AEX to consume forecasts in a form that supports executable planning today while aligning with Assortment Planning's service based forecasting architecture.  The Forecast service provides demand-channel forecasts, but AEX users still plan, buy, and execute at the fulfillment node level. Without fulfillment-channel visibility in AEX: Buy quantities will be understated. Space planning will be incorrect. Execution teams will compensate manually. Trust in AEX outputs will erode. Demand-only planning represents a future operating model, but the organization is not yet equipped—technically or behaviorally—to adopt it fully.

US Omni Tech: MerchTools: AEX Assort Product

When the AEX Fashion Line Planning experience was originally built, historical LY and LLY selling data was intended to help merchants make informed Pre-Season assortment decisions by reviewing prior performance, determining how many styles and colors to carry under each fineline, adding those to their current Line Plan in the tool, and then editing TY plan metrics (Pre-Season) or adding incremental styles and colors as needed.  In practice, Fashion merchants rely on enterprise selling history tools such as MerchOne and Scintilla**, outside of AEX Fashion,** to inform high-level assortment breadth, such as how many finelines, styles, and colors they can carry in a season. Those high-level decisions are then shared with Suppliers, Product Development, and Sourcing Partners, and detailed assortment inputs for the season are later provided back to merchants through spreadsheets from Suppliers (primarily National Brands) and internal Product Development and Sourcing partners (primarily Private Label).  Merchants use historical LY and LLY data and/or the “Add Existing” fineline functionality, along with the ability to add styles and colors in AEX Fashion Line Planning, to set up the required fineline, style, and color structure that aligns to these partner inputs and to complete the attributes needed for AEX downstream modules and execution integration, not as the primary source of Pre-Season assortment details.  As a result, Line Planning today requires significant manual consolidation and re-entry of partner inputs, increasing effort and risk of errors, and limiting the ability of AEX Fashion to serve as an integrated starting point for Pre-Season assortment planning.
AEX Fashion:  Use Assortment Planning's  Plan Creation & Plan List

Walmart requires a shared event calendar and lightweight project management system that synchronizes workflows, timelines, and milestones across MerchOne (merchants), OneSource (sourcing/technical), and SupplierOne (suppliers) in order to ensure all users—internal and external—operate on the same sequence of events and critical business milestones tied to PLM, sourcing, and product readiness.
Shared Project Tracking and Event Calendar

Walmart's early-stage product development and evaluation processes are fragmented across multiple systems: - PD develops proprietary/private brand concepts in OneSource, where merchants cannot see early work. - Designers and PD teams develop concepts in Centric, also without merchant visibility. - Merchants operate in AEX or Assortment Planning (AP), lacking visibility into upstream PD/Design intent. - Suppliers submit concepts via Supplier One or email, creating parallel "shadow repositories." Each tool maintains its own version of "potential items," blocking collaboration, duplicating effort, and preventing downstream services from operating on a single, consistent dataset. This delays merchant decisions, creates redundant concept creation, and prevents SKU-level forecasting until very late in the process.

Merchant strategy is currently defined outside of core systems (presentations, spreadsheets, meetings) and manually interpreted by downstream teams. As a result: Strategy is not consistently enforced. Design and assortment decisions drift from merchant intent. Systems reflect outputs but not intent. Changes in strategy cause rework and confusion. There is no system-level mechanism to input, version, and execute merchant strategy across OneSource and Centric.
